item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Annabelle trying and failing to end the interview was a very compelling moment for me, so the fact that O5-5 cuts the article short in the same way is just an exquisitely-crafted payoff.

Well done! A gut punch, but the sort of swift-jab kind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh… Oh I like this one a lot! It's short, but those last few lines hit really hard.

+`

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastically written, great ending, and extremely cool to get a look at the workings and psychology of the Foundation from this end. +1

Looks like I won't be sleeping for another two hours.
Very spooky (and by spooky I mean it utterly terrified me).

Also, that gut punch of an ending left me craving more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly amazing, I love this one. The interviews are great, the idea is great, and the end is incredible, it all leaves me wanting more.

item.title: Extremely Well Done
item.content:encoded: Excellently done. It’s hard to make a skip this short be this meaningful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice worldbuilding skip that brings some interesting perspectives on the morals of Foundation personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tanhony, you have been prolific in terms of output these last couple months, both in volume and quality. Another hearty +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first this just seemed like a spooky sleep paralysis ghost, but it being a sort of karmic punishment made it a lot more interesting for me.
It seems kind of unlikely that the interviewees wouldn't really understand the relationship between their experiences with 5131 and their job (i.e. the guy who operates the femur breaker gets a pain in his leg) - but it's entirely possible they do and just didn't bother saying it to the AI shrink.

I really like this, at any rate. Reminds me of the early SCPs I read when the site was way younger: short, sweet and scary.
Woo! Thank you, everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It helps somewhat, but in an official one-on-one interview it still feels like a more than noticeable pause. Even having a quick "sophia.aic: Mr. Abram?" chaser would resolve it for me.

I realize
spoilers
>>

these people are being punished relative to their duties. Femur breaker, etc. I meant who's being punished feels random. Everyone vaguely related to D-class suffering is getting sleep paralysis from the Foundation's first expendable human? I feel like there's a deeper story here about "the first link in the chain", but it doesn't fully hit the mark. Sorry Tanhony.

There really is a lot here to like in the subtle touches. The fact these events only happen once adds a nice layer of mystery, and deepens the anomaly. The worldbuilding comments from the O5 are interesting, and make sense in-universe. I'm still on novote for this one, but only just.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers?
+ show block
– hide block

i was enjoying the slow burn horror and then i realized after the O5 interview what their job titles were. really well done reveal. loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, sitting silently for a full minute seems like a stretch in an interview

I don't know if this helps this any, but these are text-based interviews, so they're sitting silently at their computer for that minute. The employees being affected by this aren't necessarily random, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever premise, but I don't know if I can get behind the execution. There isn't any clear line of reasoning linking the anomaly, the sleep paralysis, to the origin. It feels like a generic "haunting" situation, with no real stakes involved beyond spooking random Foundation employees.

The dialogue also could use a lot of tightening. The first two interviews don't feel like different people, just slightly different information. I don't get a clear separation of voice when I read them, they're both just hesitant and proud. Also, sitting silently for a full minute seems like a stretch in an interview.

I know this is a Bright challenge, but it still feels like there's a lot lacking here. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a nice article, but that last bit at the final interview did it for me. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An absolutely lovely scip to be greeted on the newly posted page with after a long break from the site. Very well-written and builds straight to the finish in a way that hits just perfectly. Doesn’t fuss with extra bits and baubles, just takes the tools it needs to get in, do its job, and get out in a way that leaves you really thinking about the SCP verse in that way most my favorites tend to.

+1 my friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's hard to write an SCP that hits it out of the park on a last line like this. Excellent stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short, sweet, exceptionally well-executed, and boy howdy are the first two adjectives in short supply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending neatly sidesteps the problem I’ve had with previous skips where the Overseers express qualms about their job or wax on about the human cost of the Foundation in unconvincing ways. It also raises some fantastic questions about what that first D-Class was tested on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love how you used a D-class personnel.

Unique and room for mystery.

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creepy! I really liked it and I hope theres some tales and cannon that springs up over "The Unknown D-Class" I love meta Skips.

have a plus one!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is my attempt at the 2011 Bright Challenge:

Write an SCP, in under 24 hours. Do not use pastebins, sandboxes, or anything else, do not ask for outside help, just, think up an idea, any idea, and write it.

I had fun writing it, but judge it as you will! :)
